---
title: "3.5 Kalman Filter as Market-Making Model"
subtitle: "PDF notes | Chapter 03. Implementing Mean Reversion Strategies"

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "NotoSerifCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    mainfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSerifCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    sansfont: "NotoSansCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    sansfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSansCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    monofont: "MapleMono-NF-CN-Regular.ttf"
    monofontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Bold.ttf"
      - "ItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Italic.ttf"
      - "BoldItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-BoldItalic.ttf"
    mathfont: "latinmodern-math.otf"
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    filecolor: blue
include-in-header:
  text: |
    \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{title}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{author}{\rmfamily}
    \addtokomafont{date}{\rmfamily}
---

# The Kalman Filter Can Estimate Fair Value for One Asset

In Section 3.4, the hidden state contained the slope and intercept linking two
assets. Chan now strips the model down to one latent variable: the current mean
price of a single asset.

The measurement equation becomes

$$
y(t) = m(t) + \varepsilon(t),
\tag{3.14}
$$

where:

- $y(t)$ is the observed transaction price
- $m(t)$ is the hidden fair value or mean price
- $\varepsilon(t)$ is measurement noise

The state transition remains a random walk:

$$
m(t) = m(t-1) + \omega(t-1).
\tag{3.15}
$$

So the fair value is assumed to drift gradually rather than stay fixed.

# The Update Rule Has the Same Logic as Before

The recursive update is

$$
m(t|t)
= m(t|t-1) + K(t)\bigl(y(t)-m(t|t-1)\bigr),
\tag{3.16}
$$

which says that the new mean estimate equals the old predicted mean plus a
fraction of the latest pricing error.

The forecast-error variance is

$$
Q(t) = \operatorname{Var}(m(t)) + V_e,
\tag{3.17}
$$

the Kalman gain is

$$
K(t) = \frac{R(t|t-1)}{R(t|t-1)+V_e},
\tag{3.18}
$$

and the state-variance update is

$$
R(t|t) = (1-K(t))R(t|t-1).
\tag{3.19}
$$

These equations matter because they separate two uncertainties:

- uncertainty about the latent fair value itself
- uncertainty about whether the observed trade price is an accurate read of
  that fair value

# The Kalman Gain Is a Trust Coefficient on the Latest Trade

In this one-asset version, the Kalman gain becomes especially intuitive.

- If $V_e$ is small, the observed trade is trusted, so $K(t)$ is high and the
  fair-value estimate moves strongly toward the new price.
- If $V_e$ is large, the observed trade is considered noisy, so $K(t)$ is small
  and the fair-value estimate moves only slightly.

This is exactly the kind of update a market maker wants. A quote should react
to information, but not every print should force a full repricing.

# Why Trade Size Belongs in the Measurement Noise

Chan's practical extension is to make $V_e$ depend on trade size. The basic
intuition is simple: a large trade contains more information about current fair
value than a tiny trade.

If trade size is denoted by $T$ and some benchmark size by $T_{\max}$, then the
measurement-noise term can shrink as $T$ grows. The qualitative effect is:

- small trades imply larger measurement noise
- large trades imply smaller measurement noise

When $T$ reaches the benchmark size, the observation is treated as highly
informative, the Kalman gain approaches one, and the updated mean moves very
close to the observed price.

# Why Chan Compares This with VWAP

Chan compares the filter with VWAP because both methods attempt to estimate a
fair trading price from observed transactions. But the Kalman version goes
beyond a simple weighted average in two ways:

- it gives more weight to larger trades
- it also gives more weight to more recent trades through recursion

So the filter behaves like a volume-and-time-weighted fair-value estimator
rather than a static average.

# Why This Counts as a Mean-Reversion Model

At first glance, the market-making version looks different from the pair-trading
version. The connection is that both models maintain a hidden fair relation and
trade the deviation between the observed price and that hidden relation.

In the pair setting, the hidden relation is the current linear fit between two
assets. In the market-making setting, the hidden relation is the latent mean of
one asset. In both cases, the tradable event is a large forecast error relative
to current uncertainty.

# Practical Interpretation

This section is useful even if one never codes a full Kalman market-making
model. It clarifies a general principle: not all trades deserve equal influence
on the current estimate of fair value. Size and recency both matter, and the
Kalman recursion provides a principled way to combine them.

# Review Checklist

- Is the hidden state the latent fair value rather than a hedge ratio?
- Is the latest trade being over-trusted or under-trusted?
- Should $V_e$ depend on trade size in this market?
- Does the recursion update fair value smoothly enough for quoting, but fast
  enough for information arrival?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [3.4 Kalman Filter as Dynamic Linear Regression]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-03/sections/04-kalman-filter-as-dynamic-linear-regression/)
- Next: [3.6 The Danger of Data Errors]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-03/sections/06-the-danger-of-data-errors/)
